clinical_history: This ECG from a 69 year old woman is most consistent with which ONE of the following diagnoses?
diagnosis: The ECG shows sinus tachycardia at about 105 bpm with several classic findings of COPD, including relatively low limb voltage and peaked P waves (~P pulmonale~) pattern, with a vertical P wave axis (about +85 degrees). The voltage in most of the precordial leads is also relatively low. Not seen is right axis of the QRS or T wave inversions in the right-mid chest leads due to right ventricular (RV) overload.  The absence of marked left atrial abnormality/intra-atrial conduction delay patterns excludes mitral stenosis or mitral regurgitation. Pulmonic stenosis generally causes a severe pressure load type of RV hypertrophy with tall R waves in V1 and V2 and right axis deviation of the QRS. Pericardial effusion and tamponade may cause low voltage and sinus tachycardia. Beat-to-beat electrical alternans is not seen here and the peaked P waves would not be explained by a pericardial syndrome alone.
